content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
December 17, 1997
Paul Bostwick
Anaheim Resort RV Park
200 W. Midway Drive
Anaheim, California 92805-6552
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-568
Dear Mr. Bostwick:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Please note that nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. The Commission will
not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).)
QUESTIONS
Is a gift of travel a reportable activity on Form 700 Schedule F? If it is reportable and
you do not know the exact value of the gift (except that it exceeds $290), what would you report?
CONCLUSIONS
Gifts of travel are reportable. A good faith determination should be made to determine
the value of the gifts. The payments should be reported on Schedule F. We cannot advise you
whether the payments are subject to gift limits because they relate to past conduct.
FACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the City of Anaheim. You are also a vice president
and former president of the Anaheim Sister Cities Association, which is an independent
501(c)(3) nonprofit association. It does not receive any monetary support from the City of
Anaheim.
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-568
Page 2
As the association president, earlier this year, you were asked to visit Chunchon City,
Korea. The Mayor of Chunchon City invited you to visit his city for the purpose of arranging a
Sister City Agreement between the two cities.
The City of Chunchon paid for your airplane ticket, hotel, and transportation costs
relating to your trip to Korea. The City of Chunchon does not do any business or have any
present connections with the City of Anaheim.
ANALYSIS
Because the subject matter ofyour questions relate to past conduct, we can only address
the questions regarding prospective reporting. I have included advice letters that may assist you
with your questions pertaining to the receipt of gifts and gift limits. Below is a general analysis
of the gift laws relating to travel.
The Act defines the term “gift” as any payment for personal purposes to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received.” (Section 82028.) The term “payment” is
defmed in Section 82044 to include “a payment, distribution, transfer, loan advance, deposit, gift,
or other rendering of money, property, services, or anything else of value, whether tangible or
intangible.”
Section 89506 provides exceptions for travel payments, advances, or reimbursements for
travel reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose or to an issue of state, national,
or governmental purpose. These payments are not prohibited or limited if the travel is in
connection with a speech given in the United States or the travel is provided by a foreign
government. (Sections 89501(c) and 89506(a); Regulation 18950.1.) If Section 89506 applies,
these payments would be reportable by you but would not be subject to the gift limits. (Section
87207; Regulation 18950.1.)
Payments for travel which do not fit within the exceptions outlined m Section 89506 are
considered gifts subject to the gift limits in Section 89503 if they are reportable by you. (Section
89506(b).) Section 89503(c) provides that no member ofa state board or commission, and no
designated employee of a state or local government agency, shall accept gifts with a total value of
more than two hundred ninety dollars ($290) in a calendar year from any single source, fthe
member or employee would be required to report the receipt ofincome or giftsfrom that source
on his or her statement ofeconomic interests.
2 As a planning commissioner, you are a public
official under the Act. (Section 82048.)
2 A source of gifts of $290 or more may also be a source of disqualifying income. (Section 87103(e).)


File No. 1-97-568
Page 3
The Act requires that every public official disclose all his or her economic interests that
could foreseeably be affected by the exercise ofthe official’s duties. (Sections 8 1002(c) and
87200.) It is your responsibility to report the source of any gift over $50 and to include the name,
address, and general description of the business activity of each source. If the source of any gift
over $50 is not attainable you should disclose that fact and the reason why that information is not
available. However, a good faith attempt at locating the actual source and amount of those gifts
is required. In addition, please note that pursuant to Section 81004 all reports and statements
required to be filed by the Act must bésigned under penalty of perjury and verified by the filer.
The verification must state that the filer has used all reasonable diligence in its preparation and
that to the best of his or her knowledge it is true and complete. (Solorio Advice Letter, No. A-
97-092.)
Section 87100 requires that public officials disqualif’ themselves from any decision
which will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on a donor of (or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of) gifts aggregating $290 or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).)
Therefore, to the extent that the benefits you described in your letter are in fact gifts, and
aggregate to $290 or more, you would be prohibited from participating or influencing any
decision which would have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the donor, or any
intermediary or agent thereof ifthose gifts were provided to, received by, or promised to you
within 12 months prior to the time that the decision is made. (Section 87 103(e).)
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division
SGC:LD:tls
Enclosures
1:\.Advice Ltrs\97568.wpd

QUESTION: Is a gift of travel a reportable activity on Form 700 Schedule F? If it is reportable and
you do not know the exact value of the gift (except that it exceeds $290), what would you report?

CONCLUSION: Gifts of travel are reportable. A good faith determination should be made to determine
the value of the gifts. The payments should be reported on Schedule F. We cannot advise you
whether the payments are subject to gift limits because they relate to past conduct.
